I'm noticing the knowledge file doesn't explicitly state that the code examples are framework-level implementations rather than application code, whereas the source material makes this distinction clear with warning comments. The text does mention that application developers don't need to manage language settings since the framework handles it, but it's less direct than saying "don't implement this way" — the phrasing is softer about what developers should actually avoid doing. findings.description: The source code examples for 'StringResourceを使用する実装例' and 'Messageを使用する実装例' both begin with an explicit warning block: `// ******** 注意 ********` / `// 直下のコードはフレームワークが行う処理であり、通常のアプリケーションでは実装する必要がない。` / `// 従って、本フレームワークを使用する場合、アプリケーション・プログラマはこのような実装を行わない。` Knowledge file s8 removes these warnings entirely and provides no compensating explanatory text, making the code examples—which include ThreadContext.setLanguage() calls—appear to be expected application code. This risks misleading an AI into advising developers to call setLanguage() themselves, which the source explicitly says is the framework's responsibility.

content.text:
One minor finding:

- **s8 (omission/minor)**: Both code examples under "オプションパラメータの国際化" carry explicit `// ******** 注意 ********` warning blocks in the source stating the code is framework-level and application programmers should not implement it. Knowledge file s8 strips these warnings without compensating text. Unlike s7 (which notes upfront that the framework sets the language), s8 has no such contextual note, leaving `ThreadContext.setLanguage()` calls looking like required application code. All other content is accurate and well-structured.

findings.description: The source code examples for 'StringResourceを使用する実装例' and 'Messageを使用する実装例' both begin with an explicit warning block: `// ******** 注意 ********` / `// 直下のコードはフレームワークが行う処理であり、通常のアプリケーションでは実装する必要がない。` / `// 従って、本フレームワークを使用する場合、アプリケーション・プログラマはこのような実装を行わない。` Knowledge file s8 removes these warnings entirely and provides no compensating explanatory text, making the code examples—which include ThreadContext.setLanguage() calls—appear to be expected application code. This risks misleading an AI into advising developers to call setLanguage() themselves, which the source explicitly says is the framework's responsibility.
